one_line_takeaway: SAGA shows that in L-infinity-bounded black-box attacks on LVLMs, WHERE and in WHAT ORDER a fixed perturbation budget is spent -- guided by a single pre-extracted cross-modal attention map and a stage-wise hotspot schedule -- matters as much as the optimization objective itself, delivering higher attack success (up to 44% relative ASR gain over the strongest baseline on Gemini models) while simultaneously improving imperceptibility across all six distortion/perceptual metrics tested.
key_finding: On Table 1 (ASR / AvgSim across ten target LVLMs), SAGA outperforms all four baselines (X-Transfer, AnyAttack, M-Attack, FOA-Attack) on every one of the ten targets. On closed-source models: Gemini-2.5-Flash ASR 0.52 vs FOA-Attack 0.35 (best baseline was M-Attack 0.36); Gemini-3-Pro ASR 0.35 vs 0.25 (FOA-Attack); GPT-4.1 ASR 0.68 vs 0.62 (FOA-Attack); GPT-5-mini ASR 0.42 vs 0.36 (FOA-Attack); Grok 4 Fast ASR 0.59 vs 0.45 (FOA-Attack). The paper reports 'up to a 44% relative ASR improvement over the strongest baseline' on Gemini models. On open-source targets SAGA also leads all five models (e.g., LLaVA-1.5-7B ASR 0.67 vs FOA-Attack 0.65; Gemma-3-4B-it 0.25 vs 0.18; Qwen3-VL-235B 0.65 vs 0.62). Table 2 shows SAGA is simultaneously best on all six imperceptibility metrics (L1=0.0269, L2=0.0330, SSIM=0.7823, PSNR=29.65, LPIPS=0.1965, BRISQUE=22.51), each better than M-Attack and FOA-Attack. Figure 5 (budget saturation ratio) shows SAGA saturates the per-pixel L-infinity budget more slowly and to a lower final level than M-Attack/FOA-Attack, despite achieving higher ASR, which the authors interpret as evidence the gains come from targeted budget allocation rather than more aggressive perturbation. Table 3 (mechanism analysis) shows SAGA has the smallest CLIP feature distance (0.4848) among methods yet is the only one with a statistically significant Pearson correlation between feature distance and attack success (r=0.118, p<0.001), vs M-Attack (r=0.041, not significant) and FOA-Attack (r=0.055, not significant). Table 4 (ablation) shows removing the stage-wise sequence (single-hotspot variant, same attention map) drops average ASR from 0.49 to 0.35 across four targets (Llama 4, Grok 4, Gemini 2.5, GPT 5-mini). Figure 7 shows hotspot region selection beats random and coldspot selection on Gemini-2.5-Flash (51.8% vs 47.2% vs 46.2%) and Qwen3-VL-235B (64.8% vs 62.9% vs 60.5%). Figure 6 shows Qwen3-VL-8B is the best attention extractor among LLaVA-7B/LLaVA-13B/Qwen3-VL-8B/ensemble, and that ensembling extractors does not help.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is SAGA (Stage-wise Attention-Guided Attack), a black-box, transfer-based targeted adversarial attack on Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) that improves how a fixed L-infinity perturbation budget is spatially allocated. Rather than proposing a new optimization objective, SAGA adds a region-selection-and-sequencing layer on top of existing transfer attacks: it extracts a single cross-modal attention map once from an open-source LVLM (Qwen3-VL-8B by default) before optimization, uses it to build an ordered sequence of N=10 stages x k=3 hotspots (with an IoU<0.3 de-duplication constraint) ranked from most- to next-most salient region, and then restricts perturbation updates at each optimization stage to the currently scheduled hotspot while keeping the adversarial image within the global L-infinity ball (epsilon=16/255). This is motivated by two empirical analyses the paper itself runs: (1) a sensitivity study on Qwen3-VL-8B showing regional cross-modal attention is positively correlated with adversarial loss sensitivity (Pearson r=0.486, Spearman=0.491, N=1000 sampled regions), and (2) a softmax-redistribution analysis (with a first-order derivation in their Appendix D) showing that when perturbations are localized to a hotspot, attention released from that hotspot flows preferentially toward regions proportional to their pre-attack attention mass, validated with Pearson r=0.409, p=2e-70 between predicted and actual regional attention changes.
threat_model: White/attacker-perspective, black-box targeted evasion/misdescription attack: an attacker perturbs a source image under an L-infinity budget (epsilon=16/255, step size 1/255, 300 iterations) so that a target LVLM's generated caption/description is steered toward an attacker-chosen target text, without any access to the target model's parameters, gradients, or attention maps -- only a single open-source LVLM (used purely to extract one attention map before the attack begins) is needed. The attack is evaluated against both closed-source commercial LVLMs (Gemini-2.5-Flash, Gemini-3-Pro-Preview, GPT-4.1, GPT-5-mini, Grok 4 Fast) and open-source LVLMs (LLaVA-1.5-7B, Gemma-3-4B-it, Llama-4-Maverick, Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B-Instruct, Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B-Instruct), using CLIP surrogate models (ViT-B/16, ViT-B/32, ViT-g-14-laion2B) for the transfer-based optimization objective.
limitations: The paper itself notes (Section 5.2 / Appendix F.6) that SAGA is not specifically designed to be robust to input-transformation defenses or diffusion-purification-style defenses (DPS), though it states a simple EOT variant can be applied orthogonally to add robustness -- meaning out-of-the-box SAGA's robustness to active defenses is not the paper's main claim and is only lightly explored. Success is measured via an LLM-judge similarity threshold (gpt-oss-20b judging similarity >0.5) rather than a human study, which is a common but imperfect proxy for 'attacker-specified content' being achieved. The attention extractor is fixed and extracted once before optimization from a single open-source model per run; the paper shows the choice of extractor matters (Figure 6) and that ensembling does not help, suggesting the method's transfer benefit is somewhat contingent on the specific open-source model chosen.

datasets_used: NIPS 2017 Adversarial Attacks and Defenses Competition dataset (1,000 source images, resized to 224x224x3)
MSCOCO validation set (1,000 images used to generate target captions via Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct)
quantitative_results: Table 1 ASR, Gemini-2.5-Flash: SAGA 0.52 vs FOA-Attack 0.35 vs M-Attack 0.36
Table 1 ASR, Gemini-3-Pro: SAGA 0.35 vs FOA-Attack 0.25
Table 1 ASR, GPT-4.1: SAGA 0.68 vs FOA-Attack 0.62
Table 1 ASR, GPT-5-mini: SAGA 0.42 vs FOA-Attack 0.36
Table 1 ASR, Grok 4 Fast: SAGA 0.59 vs FOA-Attack 0.45
Up to 44% relative ASR improvement over strongest baseline on Gemini models (paper's own claim)
Table 1 open-source: LLaVA-1.5-7B SAGA ASR 0.67 vs FOA-Attack 0.65; Gemma-3-4B-it SAGA 0.25 vs FOA-Attack 0.18; Qwen3-VL-235B SAGA 0.65 vs FOA-Attack 0.62
Table 2 imperceptibility: SAGA L1=0.0269, L2=0.0330, SSIM=0.7823, PSNR=29.65, LPIPS=0.1965, BRISQUE=22.51 -- best on all six metrics vs X-Transfer, AnyAttack, M-Attack, FOA-Attack
Sensitivity analysis (Figure 2): Pearson r=0.486, Spearman=0.491, N=1000 regions, on Qwen3-VL-8B
Redistribution validation (Appendix D per main text): Pearson r=0.409, p=2e-70

Table 3: CLIP feature distance -- SAGA 0.4848, M-Attack 0.4922, FOA-Attack 0.4957; correlation with attack success -- SAGA r=0.118 (p<0.001), M-Attack r=0.041 (n.s., p=0.191), FOA-Attack r=0.055 (n.s., p=0.080)
Table 4 stage-wise vs single-hotspot ablation: average ASR across Llama4/Grok4/Gemini2.5/GPT5-mini falls from 0.49 (SAGA) to 0.35 (single-stage)
Figure 7 hotspot vs random vs coldspot: Gemini-2.5-Flash 51.8% vs 47.2% vs 46.2%; Qwen3-VL-235B 64.8% vs 62.9% vs 60.5%
